sentences.text: -- Constantly.
I was watching the film.
Actually, the film was better than I originally thought.
I though it was-- should I-- OK, you got things going?
Well, Randy dropped off the video and I shoved it in my VCR.
And I see my picture, and then--
My name-- full name would be Iceek Miller, or Ichock.
It comes from the Hebrew.
And--
--miller.
Ichock, you asked me it very quickly, because I never spelled my name, Ichock.
I-- it's I-C-H-O-C-K, Ichock, but they called me Iceek, would be I-C-E-E-K.
Miller, M-I-L-L-E-R.
No.
No.
Except the Irvin, I was named by my cousins.
I wanted my middle name, which would be Jacob.
She said, we have too many Jacobs in our family, so Irwin.
I couldn't even pronounce it.
It started with Irwin, and then, finally it became Irvin, because I couldn't pronounce the W.
So I'm Irvin or Irv from then on.
Yes.
I was born in Targowica, Poland, just a small townlet in eastern Poland.
Targowica was spelled T-A-R-G-O-W-I-C-A.
It's-- if you translate it, it's like 'commercial point,' what it meant.
It was an interesting little town with hills and two rivers.
I was born July 12, 1929.
My father's name was Edel.
My mother's name was Chana.
My mother's maiden name was Pressman.
The town-- from my dad used to talk about it and my mom, constantly the wars.
Even before World War I, there was always battles.
It's kind of a strategic area, because of the hills and the rivers.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's all I remember.
I never was certain how many.
I would guess about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably 300 families, Jewish families.
That would be an educated guess.
There were some Poles and there was some Ukrainians, but mostly Jewish.
We had two synagogues.
And there was two churches, the Catholic church and a-- and a Russian Orthodox Church.
Actually, I don't know if it's Ukrainian Orthodox.
Mostly Ukrainians were going to that church.
My oldest brother, was-- we called him Schulich, Israel.
And my second brother was Abraham.
And my third brother was Zindel.
And then was me, Iccek, and my daughter, Rose.
And late in the life of my parents, right before the war, a little brother called Benzion was born.
And I know my oldest brother was 20 when my little brother was born, so it was a surprise.
I think my parents were not very happy about that.
Yes.
Right near us was my uncle and aunt and two cousins.
That was my mother's brother, Uncle Meir, and Aunt Perl and two cousins.
No.
I-- I don't recall my grandparents.
I mean, they left-- two of my grandparents left for the United States way before I was born.
They came here in 1922.
And my father's mother died in our house.
My-- and I was talking to my brother, and I said, I think I remember an old lady.
And it's just like a dream.
I'm not certain about it.
He says, well, he says, my-- you know, our grandmother lived with us.
But nothing else I remember, when she died or anything like that.
But I thought I do remember.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] I must have been, I don't know, maybe three years old or two years old.
So--
My mother's father and mother.
They came to Columbus.
They lived in Columbus.
And we also had two of my uncles and two aunts migrated, in the year-- I think my grandparents came in '22 with my two aunts.
The uncles came before that.
A great uncle came before the 19th century.
And he brought, slowly, one at a time.
[SIGHS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a child, as a young kid, I remember we had some good times.
We used to play a lot.
We had a lot of little friends.
We were constantly outside, always playing games and doing some things.
It wasn't an unhappy childhood.
I was happy.
My parents were wonderful people.
And the brothers and-- we got along.
I was always proud of my older brothers.
And it's just unfortunate that it ended so soon, at the age of 12, things-- or the age of 10, the war in '39 started, and things were never the same from then on.
So up to 10 years old, what I remember was just fine.
We were not-- we were poor people.
I mean, by the standard of that little town, we were not, because there were a lot of people had a lot less than we did, so we considered ourselves fortunate that way.
There was-- one of the games I remember was a lot like baseball, but we used a small rubber ball, almost like a racquetball.
And we used sticks instead of bats.
And it was very close to baseball.
And there's other little games.
In summertime we would just-- did different things.
We played soccer.
We never had a good ball in soccer, so we made our own.
And there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brothers played volleyball.
Winter sports was fun, because we're right near hills.
We did some homemade skis, did some skiing, homemade skates.
Right by the river, so we did some skating.
And I did also a little fishing.
I love fishing, because that was so close.
That was about, probably, 200 yards to the river, maybe less.
And--
The skis, we-- we were-- there was a-- a blacksmith shop.
And I always used to hang out there, always tried to help them.
So I would get things done.
Yeah.
We made our own fishing pole.
[SIGHS] There was a place that we used to cut the wood down for the pole.

And we would go in the market and the horses would just be in the market and we'd pull horses' hair out of their tail and able to ply, like, four strings together, and tie them up, and use a goose feather.
And we would have the line go through that goose feather and put a hook, which was not expensive.
I mean, for a nickel, probably-- or 5 groszy, which was worth a penny, we'd get like three hooks.
And we would catch flies at home in the kitchen and put them in a-- a matchbox, .
And we use that, or dough, just kind of-- or if you wanted bigger fish, we would dig worms.
So there was-- we always kept busy as kids.
Used to catch little fish, and always I wanted my mom to make those, fry them, because I loved them.
And there were times that she did it.
She was always so busy, you know, feeding such a big family.
But at times, they were too little to mess with.
But she used to do that.
House was kind of a-- it's all one floor.
We lived like by the street, by a-- by a road that used to go to the police station.
And to the-- there was also a public bath.
People-- It's a big house.
It was spread out.
We had a picket fence around it.
We also have attached barns, where my dad would keep the horses.
And-- and it had a huge-- we call it veranda.
It's like a porch facing the river.
It was very interesting.
And-- and we used to play underneath that.
We used to play under the bridge for some reason.
I don't know.
There was always mysterious things happening under the bridge.
There was a barn attached to-- yes, to the house.
Yes.
We rented part of the house to some other people.
One was a veterinarian.
And then later on, there was someone else.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
My father was a-- dealt with horses, used to buy and sell horses.
He traded horses.
That was his business, yes.
He traveled just about every day to the markets.
And they were just-- not in our little town.
There were-- were none.
But some of the major cities he would travel to.
He did for all kinds of horses.
He had some-- occasionally would have racing horses, show horses, and also to some farmers, and mostly farmers who were very large farmers, that they-- you know.
Small farmers always somehow managed to raise their own, or something, just like-- every small farmer would have two horses.
But some of these huge, huge, farms, they'd work mostly with horses.
And he would supply them and then buy some back.
Yes.
I used to ride horses.
We all-- we all did, bareback.
I must have ridden horses the age of three or four.
Yes.
My dad, actually, during the First World War, he was a cavalryman, so he used to-- people used to admire watching my dad riding horses.
He was just very good at it.
I guess he was the mailman, at one point.
He delivered mail.
And we used to always watch-- enjoy watching dad.
And I don't know whether he taught us.
He was too busy doing things.
But we learned on our own.
My brother, he was extremely good.
He was sort of a daredevil.
He tried horses that everybody was scared to come near them.
He would try to ride them.
That was Abraham.
He was a daredevil of all kinds.
He would jump from the bridge into the water.
He would walk on the ice when it was like a half an inch thick, and it would crack under his-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was quite a guy.
I-- I remember there was some seven, eight horses.
But there was a place that he would have somewhere else that he would keep horses.
But in our barn was at the most I think, maybe seven or eight.
Yes.
I did a lot.
In fact, I did most of them without Dad asking me.
I always-- it seemed like I always tried to please my dad, and I was a hard-working kid.
I used to bring water from the river, fill up the barrels, feed the horses, feed the cow.
We had a-- we had a cow.
And so, always worked.
I just always remember working.
I think I worked harder than my older brother.
Oh, they were always great.
They were just always wonderful, because the food was so good and different and a lot.
And it was just great.
I have good memories about Passover was a wonderful holiday, or any holiday, because-- I guess, mostly for the food.
We did go-- of course, we all went to synagogue.
And I guess Dad would stay home.
It was a nice-- you know, we did some things with him.
[SIGHS] And yeah, some good memories.
I think Passover, because there was so much good food.
And-- and it lasted so long, seven days.
Well, I was always-- we were always reminded of.
We just kind of knew about it.
I don't think it was-- we lived mostly among Jewish people, but there were some Gentiles.
And on occasions, we would play with some of the Gentile kids.
And then occasionally there would arise the [NON-ENGLISH], Jew to Palestine, things like that.
But this was not ongoing.
I mean, it happened on occasions.
We fought back.
We had battles.
Kids were fighting with each other, and then we made up.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] At that time, it wasn't-- it wasn't bad, as far as I can remember.
But we knew there was anti-Semitism.
Yes.
Now, if you-- the war, I remember also in 1939, when the war-- let's see. 1939 was the pact between Stalin and Hitler.
And the Russians marched in in 1939 to our part of the country.
And-- and the war actually started in-- with the-- Russia and Germany in 1941.
And this was June the 22nd.
I remember very vividly that day, yes.
I was-- it was on a Sunday.
We were gathered in a-- in school for was some important official supposed to make a speech.
And they were interrupted by announcement that a war started.
Actually, in fact, there was a song written in-- right-- during the War.
[INAUDIBLE].
Exactly at 4 o'clock, you know, I remember this song at one time.
So that date is kind of-- [SIGHS]
Stayed with me.
For reason-- I mean, this was on a Sunday, which there was no school.
But it was some kind of a commissar was supposed to come in.
And-- and there was an important meeting.
And I was, at that time, a Pioneer, and I think I had a-- I was the head of a small group, like 12 kids.
And then the-- then the next thing was the Komsomol, and others.
So-- and I don't remember what the speech was about.
But like I said, it was quickly interrupted, because of the-- in the afternoon, Sunday afternoon.
Pioneer was like Boy Scouts.
You know, you-- but-- but it's-- it's a little different.
I think the Russian indoctrination, you start with the Pioneers and the Komsomol, and then maybe later on, the Communist Party.
So-- but we-- you know, like Boy Scouts.
Well, we dispersed and called off.
And-- and from then on, there was no more school.
And within a very short time, within maybe a week, the Germans already went through our part of the-- our little town.
At that time, when they were coming in, maybe a day before, two days, my brother Abraham, my dad insisted that he runs further east, not to stay here, because it was dangerous.
They thought, you know, if anything, they're going to do harm to young-- young men, especially of army age.
My oldest brother was already in the Russian army.
He was in officers' training school.
So he went in 1940, just a year before the war started.
So after that, we-- we lost contact with him.
There were no more letters.
I mean, when the war-- yeah.
When the war started, everything was interrupted, so-- [SIGHS]
No.
He volunteered.
He wanted to go to become an officer.
Yes.
OK.
Right.
When the-- when the Germans were coming towards, they were very close, he wanted him to run, to run away.
And he did.
I guess he too, wanted, cause he told me years later.
[SIGHS] I remember just vaguely.
I do remember when he left, yes.
I mean, he left so quickly.
I mean, he just grabbed something.
He didn't carry much with him.
He just took off.
I mean, it was not-- goodbye, and that was it.
He took off.
They were just scared.
I mean, the Germans could be any-- any-- any hour.
Yeah.
We just-- immediately, once he left, that was-- that was it.
That was all.
There were some units.
I remember there was-- there was-- the first I've seen was on motorcycles, and then, of course, army Jeeps and trucks.
They just went through.
They didn't-- they spent one or two days, occupied some of the areas, and then they just went through.
And then they-- we didn't see any Germans.
There were mostly Ukrainians.
There were a lot of nationalistic groups who-- who hated the Russians.

And they hoped that Germany would give them independence in that part of the-- And they were-- and they were actually working with the Germans, you know, hoping that that's what's going to happen, so they did a lot of dirty work for the Germans arresting Jews.
They were.
At the beginning, they were not soldiers.
There were no soldiers.
But they were young, mostly younger people.
But they quickly organized.
Germans gave them uniforms and some training.
The Germans?
Well, the Ukrainians were always around there.
In fact, a lot of these people knew my parents, knew my dad.

And there was a time when they-- some of these people would come every night-- they made us-- and get us out of the house until 1:00 in the morning, line us up in front of the wall like they're going to kill us.
And they threatened my dad constantly because of-- of my two older brothers.
One was in the army and the other one went with the Russians.
So-- so they would do that for several-- for a couple weeks.
If it's not every night, it's every other night, point the guns at us.
We were scared to death.
We thought every time they do that, they're going to kill us.
And my dad, actually, was known as a good person.
I mean, most people who knew him, I mean, Gentile, Jew or Gentile, they had a lot of respect for my dad.
He was just a good person.
But they threatened him only because of my brothers.
They were young brothers, so it was like a-- you know, it's a sin to have-- and being in the army, I would imagine, has something to do with it.
OK.
